elements.sent-2411-001: We develop the theory of the non-adiabatic phonon self-energy arising from coupling to electrons with finite linewidths using the spectral representation of Green's functions.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our formalism naturally includes the contribution from the intra-band electronic transitions at all electron wavevectors to the phonon linewidths, which is forbidden for zone-center optical phonons if infinitesimal electron linewidths are used.
elements.sent-2411-003: As a proof of principle, we use the theory to calculate the linewidth of the double-degenerate, zone-center optical $E_{\rm 2g}$ phonons of graphene as a function of the chemical potential.
elements.sent-2411-004: The calculated phonon linewidths obtained with finite electron linewidths differ significantly from those obtained with infinitesimal electron linewidths even at low temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-005: Intra-band electronic transitions play an important role in making this difference.
elements.sent-2411-006: Moreover, only the results accounting for the finite electron linewidths are in quantitative agreement with available experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-007: The presented formalism is suitable for first-principles calculations.
elements.sent-2411-008: The phonon energy is conventionally obtained from density-functional perturbation theory calculations~\cite{2001Baroni_RMP_DFPT within the adiabatic, Born-Oppenheimer approximation.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Many-body interaction effects beyond the adiabatic approximation can be incorporated as energy-dependent, complex self-energy arising from electron-phonon interactions.
elements.sent-2411-010: This self-energy is called the non-adiabatic phonon self-energy~\cite{2017Giustino_RMP_ElPh .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Non-adiabatic phonon self-energy plays an important role in materials in which the energy difference between occupied and empty electronic states is comparable to phonon energies.
elements.sent-2411-012: Some of the early examples of first-principles calculations on the imaginary part of the non-adiabatic phonon self-energy arising from electron-phonon interactions are \citet{PhysRevB.19.3708, PhysRevB.57.11276, PhysRevLett.90.095506, PhysRevB.73.155426 .}
elements.sent-2411-013:
In previous first-principles studies, the imaginary part of the non-adiabatic (NA) phonon self-energy was calculated as suggested by ~\citet{1972Allen_PRB_phonon_linewidth :  egin{widetext  egin{equation}\label{eq:Pi_omega_non_adiabatic_Allen} {m Im\,} \Pi^{m NA}_{\qnu}(\omega_\qnu)=-\pi\,\sum_{mn}\,\int  rac{d{ f k}}{\Omega_{m BZ}} |g_{mn
u}({ f k},{ f q})|^2\,\left[f(\varepsilon_
k)-f(\varepsilon_\mkq)ight]\, \delta (\varepsilon_
k+\omega_\qnu-\varepsilon_\mkq)\,,\end{equation} \end{widetext where, $f(\varepsilon)=1/\left[e^{(\varepsilon-\mu)/k_{m B T +1ight]$ is the Fermi-Dirac occupation factor at temperature $T$ and chemical potential $\mu$, and $\Omega_{m BZ}$ is the volume of the Brillouin zone.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014:
The electron-phonon coupling constant is given by  egin{equation}\label{eq:g} g_{mn
u}({ f k},{ f q}) = \left<\mkq\left|\Delta V_\qnuight|
kight>\,,\end{equation} where $\left|
kight>$ indicates the electronic state with wavevector ${ f k}$, band index $m$ and energy $\varepsilon_
k$, and $\Delta V_\qnu$ is the change in the self-consistent potential due to a phonon with branch index $
u$, wavevector ${ f q}$, and energy $\omega_\qnu$.
elements.sent-2411-015: The energy conservation condition \begin{equation}\label{eq:energy_conservation} \varepsilon_\mkq=\varepsilon_\nk+\omega_\qnu\end{equation} was imposed in calculating the imaginary part of the non-adiabatic phonon self-energy because of the energy delta function in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Pi_omega_non_adiabatic_Allen .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Therefore, this condition is unnecessary if we consider finite electron linewidths.
elements.sent-2411-017: In some of the model studies, it was shown that such finite electron linewidths play an important role in the non-adiabatic phonon self-energy~\cite{PhysRevB.96.054515, PhysRevB.98.041112 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, a first-principles theory for non-adiabatic phonon self-energy taking into account the finite electron linewidths has yet to be developed.
elements.sent-2411-019: This paper presents the theory for non-adiabatic phonon self-energy arising from coupling to electrons with finite linewidths.
elements.sent-2411-020: In Sec.~\ref{sec:methods , we present the theory and method for first-principles calculations of non-adiabatic phonon self-energy, and in Sec.~\ref{sec:results we apply the method to graphene at low temperatures.}}

elements.sent-2411-042: The factor 2 difference between Eq.~\eqref{eq:Pi_omega_limit3 and Eqs.~(145) and~(146) of Ref.~\citealp{2017Giustino_RMP_ElPh is that the latter considered non-magnetic, non-spinor cases and confined the band summation to only over the bands of one kind of spin.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: The electronic band velocity, the energy of the zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonon energy, and electron-phonon coupling constants were taken from the model in Tab.~I of Ref.~\citealp{2014Park_NL_graphene_resistivity ; this model was made from first-principles calculations and reproduced the experimentally measured intrinsic electrical resistivity of graphene~\cite{2014Park_NL_graphene_resistivity and was also applied to the calculation of the electronic thermal conductivity of graphene~\cite{2016Kim_NL_graphene_thermal_conductivity .}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Following this model, we included two linear energy bands with band indices $+1$ and $-1$ (for both $m$ and $n$), whose energy is above and below the Dirac point energy, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-045: For the numerical calculation of the wavevector integration, we included states whose energy is between $-4$~eV and $4$~eV, where the Dirac point energy is set to zero.
elements.sent-2411-046: The momentum integration in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Pi_omega3 along the radial direction from any one of the two Dirac points was performed with the energy range being divided into 20,000 uniformly sampled points, and that along the circumferential direction was performed analytically.}
elements.sent-2411-047: If we account for the spin and valley degrees of freedom, the phonon linewidth [Eq.~\eqref{eq:Gamma ] of the degenerate zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonons of graphene reduces to \begin{widetext \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:Gamma_graphene} \Gamma_{E_{\rm 2g}} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{b\,\bar{g}}{\hbar\,v_{\rm F}}\right)^2 \sum_{m,\,n=\,\{-1,+1\}}\,\int_0^{4~{\rm eV}} d\varepsilon\,\varepsilon\,{\rm Re\,}\left\{...\right\}\,,\end{eqnarray} \end{widetext where $b$ is the carbon-carbon bond length in graphene, $v_{\rm F}$ is the band velocity around the Dirac point, and $\bar{g}$ is the angle-averaged electron-phonon coupling constant and is given by $\bar{g =\frac{3 {2}\,\eta\,\sqrt{\frac{\hbar {2M_{\rm C}\omega_\Gamma^{{\rm E _{2g }$ using the notation in Tab.~I of Ref.~\citealp{2014Park_NL_graphene_resistivity .}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Also, ${\rm Re\,}\left\{...\right\}$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Gamma_graphene is obtained from ${\rm Re\,}\left\{...\right\}$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Pi_omega3 by replacing $\omega$ with $\omega_\qnu=0.2$~eV, $\varepsilon_\nk$ and $\varepsilon_\mkq$ with $m\,\varepsilon$ and $n\,\varepsilon$, respectively, and $\Sigma{'' _\mkq$ and $\Sigma{'' _\nk$ with $\Sigma{'' (m\,\varepsilon)$ and $\Sigma{'' (n\,\varepsilon)$, respectively.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Here, the function $\Sigma{'' (\varepsilon)$ is set to a good approximation to give the magnitude of the imaginary part of the experimentally measured electron self-energy~\cite{2007Boswick_NatPhys_graphene_ARPES , which is in good agreement with first-principles calculations~\cite{2007Park_PRL_graphene_eph, 2009Park_PRL_graphene_linewidth, 2009Park_NL_graphene_ARPES : \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:ImSigma_graphene} \Sigma{''}(\varepsilon) =\nnnl \begin{cases} 0.054\,|\varepsilon-0.2~\text{eV}|+0.01~\text{eV} \text{if}\,\varepsilon >\mu+0.2~\text{eV} 0.054\,|\varepsilon+0.2~\text{eV}|+0.01~\text{eV} \text{if}\,\varepsilon<\mu-0.2~\text{eV} 0.01~\text{eV} \text{otherwise}\,. \end{cases}\end{eqnarray} Note that the calculated phonon linewidths are not very sensitive to $\Sigma{'' (\varepsilon)$ as long as it approximately describes the imaginary part of the measured electron self-energy.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{./Fig_10K_experiment_with_schematic.eps} \caption{ (a) Linewidth [full width at half maximum (FWHM)] of the zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonons of graphene vs. chemical potential at $T=10$~K. Theoretical results were obtained according to Eq.~\eqref{eq:Gamma_graphene} with finite electron linewidths [Eq.~\eqref{eq:ImSigma_graphene}] (solid or red curve) or with infinitesimal electron linewidths - we assumed 0.001~eV linewidths - (dashed or blue curve) from Eq.~\eqref{eq:Pi_omega3}. We took the experimental data from Ref.~\citealp{2007Yan_PRL_graphene_phonon_linewidth} and shifted them by $(-3)$~cm$^{-1}$ to account for other phonon scattering mechanisms than electron-phonon interactions (see text). (b) Schematic showing that a zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonon in graphene can decay through electronic transitions at low temperatures only if $|\mu|<\omega_\qnu/2$ if the electron linewidths are infinitesimal. } \label{fig:graphene_experiment}
elements.sent-2411-050: Figure~\ref{fig:graphene_experiment (a) shows that, at low temperatures, the linewidth of the degenerate zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonons of graphene calculated with infinitesimal electron linewidths is constant if the chemical potential is between $-0.1$~eV and $+0.1$~eV from the Dirac point energy and is zero outside this window.}
elements.sent-2411-051: The reason for this behavior is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:graphene_experiment (b).}
elements.sent-2411-052: At low temperatures, if $|\mu|<\omega_\qnu/2=0.1$~eV, a phonon with energy $\omega_\qnu=0.2$~eV, can decay by an electronic transition; however, if $|\mu|>\omega_\qnu/2$, this transition is Pauli-blocked, and the phonon is not absorbed by vertical electronic transitions.
elements.sent-2411-053: Our calculated results for zero electron linewidths are in good agreement with previous first-principles calculations~\cite{2006Lazzeri_PRL_graphene_Kohn_anomaly,2008Park_NL_graphene_eph .}
elements.sent-2411-054: However, the results calculated with finite electron linewidths [Eqs.~\eqref{eq:Gamma_graphene and~\eqref{eq:ImSigma_graphene ] are different from these results: Even in graphene with high charge density, the scattering rate is finite, because the energy conservation [Eq.~\eqref{eq:energy_conservation ] with sharply defined electronic energies is no longer required; hence, all electronic states contribute to the linewidth of the $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonons.}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: To compare the calculated phonon linewidths with the experimental data in~\citet{2007Yan_PRL_graphene_phonon_linewidth , we shifted the experimental data by $-3$~cm$^{-1}$ to match the phonon linewidth obtained from computations at zero charge density because there are other scattering mechanisms, which are not very sensitive to the charge density such as the phonon-phonon scattering, for phonon linewidths measured from experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-056: We note that~\citet{2007Bonini_PRL_graphene_phonon have shown that the 3-phonon scattering processes at low temperatures add 1.65~cm$^{-1}$ to the total phonon linewidth.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Therefore, other scattering mechanisms, such as phonon-impurity scattering and higher-order scattering processes, are responsible for the remaining shift in the experimental data, $3-1.65=1.35$~cm$^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-058: Notably, the experimental data on the linewidths vs. chemical potential are much more in agreement with the calculations obtained with finite electron linewidths.
elements.sent-2411-059: Our calculations with finite electronic linewidths even reproduce the {\it increase in the measured linewidth with chemical potential at higher charge density, which is due to intra-band electronic transitions as we explain below and show in Fig.~\ref{fig:graphene_band_resolution .}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.98\columnwidth]{./Fig_10K_band_resolution.eps} \caption{ Band-resolved phonon linewidth [each summand of Eq.~\eqref{eq:Gamma_graphene}] calculated with finite electron linewidths. $m$ and $n$ indicate, respectively, the electronic bands {\it after} and {\it before} absorbing a zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonon of graphene. All four contributions amount to the sold or red curve in Fig.~\ref{fig:graphene_experiment}. } \label{fig:graphene_band_resolution}
elements.sent-2411-060: To better understand the computational results, we divide the total phonon linewidth into the contributions from different electronic transitions, i.\,e.\,, with different $m$ and $n$ pairs.
elements.sent-2411-061: The results are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:graphene_band_resolution .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The $n=-1$ to $m=+1$ transition, i.\,e.\,, the process in which a negative energy electronic state absorbs a phonon and makes a transition to a positive energy electronic state, is by far dominant in the phonon linewidths.
elements.sent-2411-063: Also, this contribution does not vanish even if $|\mu|>\omega_\qnu/2$.
elements.sent-2411-064: However, the most interesting result is that the intra-band contributions, i.e., processes involving the electronic transition in the same band ($m=n$), increase with $|\mu|$ and are dominant at high charge densities.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{./Fig_Temp_variation.eps} \caption{ Linewidth of the zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonons of graphene at different temperatures calculated according to Eq.~\eqref{eq:Gamma_graphene} with finite electron linewidths [Eqs.~\eqref{eq:ImSigma_graphene}] and without finite electron linewidths - we assumed 0.001~eV linewidths. } \label{fig:temp_variations}
elements.sent-2411-065: Finally, Fig.~\ref{fig:temp_variations shows the calculated phonon linewidths at different temperatures.}
elements.sent-2411-066: The linewidths vs. chemical potential curves obtained with infinitesimal electron linewidths become smoother as the temperature increases.
elements.sent-2411-067: The results obtained with finite electron linewidths show additional features and, in general, vary more smoothly with $|\mu|$, and the phonon linewidth increases with $|\mu|$ in the high-charge-density regime.
elements.sent-2411-068: In this paper, we presented the theory for calculating non-adiabatic phonon self-energy arising from the coupling to electrons with finite linewidths.
elements.sent-2411-069: The theory naturally introduces the contribution to phonon linewidth from intra-band electronic transitions for all electronic states, not just the ones satisfying the energy conservation condition upon phonon absorption.
elements.sent-2411-070: As a proof of principle, we demonstrated that the measured zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonon linewidth vs. chemical potential behavior of graphene at low temperatures can be quantitatively explained only if the finite electron linewidths are considered.
elements.sent-2411-071: Remarkably, we find that the intra-band electronic transitions, which are forbidden in this example if infinitesimal electronic linewidths are used, contribute significantly to the self-energy of the zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonons of graphene.
elements.sent-2411-072: Our formalism can be easily implemented in existing software for computing electron-phonon interactions from first principles.
elements.sent-2411-073: We expect the theory will be necessary to interpret experimental data on materials whose phonon energy scale is non-negligible compared to the energy scale of low-energy electronic transitions, which are materials of contemporary interest.
elements.sent-2411-074: This work was supported by Korean NRF No-2023R1A2C1007297.
elements.sent-2411-075: Computational resources were provided by KISTI Supercomputing Center (Grant No. KSC-2023-CRE-0533).. \end{acknowledgments.}
elements.sent-2411-078: We will make use of the following identity frequently: \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:Matsubara_sum_digamma} \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(ip_l + a)(ip_l + b)}\nnnl = \frac{\beta}{(2\pi i)^2} \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(l + \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\, a)(l + \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\,b )}\nnnl = \frac{\beta}{2\pi i} \frac{\psi(\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\,a) - \psi(\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\,b)}{a-b}\,,\end{eqnarray} which follows from \begin{equation}\label{eq:digamma_equality} \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(l+a)(l+b)} = \frac{\psi(a) - \psi(b)}{a-b}\,,\end{equation} which again follows from the definition of the digamma function in Eq.~\eqref{eq:digamma .}
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Matsubara_sum_digamma} \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(ip_l + a)(ip_l + b)}\nnnl = \frac{\beta}{(2\pi i)^2} \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(l + \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\, a)(l + \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\,b )}\nnnl = \frac{\beta}{2\pi i} \frac{\psi(\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\,a) - \psi(\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\,b)}{a-b}\,,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-079: Equations~\eqref{eq:Matsubara_sum_digamma and~\eqref{eq:digamma_equality correspond to Eqs.~(S44) and~(S45) of Ref.~\citealp{2024LihmSCEL .}}}

elements.sent-2411-114: Here, the function $\Sigma{'' (\varepsilon)$ is set to a good approximation to give the magnitude of the imaginary part of the experimentally measured electron self-energy~\cite{2007Boswick_NatPhys_graphene_ARPES , which is in good agreement with the first-principles calculations~\cite{2007Park_PRL_graphene_eph, 2009Park_PRL_graphene_linewidth, 2009Park_NL_graphene_ARPES : \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:ImSigma_graphene} \Sigma{''}(\varepsilon) =\nnnl \begin{cases} 0.054\,|\varepsilon-0.2~\text{eV}|+0.01~\text{eV} \text{if}\,\varepsilon >\mu+0.2~\text{eV} 0.054\,|\varepsilon+0.2~\text{eV}|+0.01~\text{eV} \text{if}\,\varepsilon<\mu-0.2~\text{eV} 0.01~\text{eV} \text{otherwise}\,. \end{cases}\end{eqnarray} Note that the calculated phonon linewidths are not very sensitive to $\Sigma{'' (\varepsilon)$ as long as it approximates the imaginary part of the measured electron self-energy.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Note also that the electron linewidths depend appreciably on the quality of the sample~\cite{2007Yan_PRL_graphene_phonon_linewidth,PhysRevB.91.205413,PhysRevB.107.075420 .}
elements.sent-2411-116: Therefore, the phonon linewidths may also depend on the sample quality.
elements.sent-2411-117: However, the results calculated with finite electron linewidths [Eqs.~\eqref{eq:Gamma_graphene and~\eqref{eq:ImSigma_graphene ] are different from these results: Even in graphene with high charge density, the scattering rate is finite, because energy conservation [Eq.~\eqref{eq:energy_conservation ] with sharply defined electronic energies is no longer required; hence, all electronic states contribute to the linewidth of the $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonons.}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: To compare the calculated phonon linewidths with the experimental data in~\citet{2007Yan_PRL_graphene_phonon_linewidth , we shifted the experimental data by $-3$~cm$^{-1}$ to match the phonon linewidth obtained from computations at zero charge density because there are other scattering mechanisms, which are not very sensitive to the charge density such as phonon-phonon scattering, for phonon linewidths measured from experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-119: We note that~\citet{2007Bonini_PRL_graphene_phonon have shown that 3-phonon scattering processes at low temperatures add 1.65~cm$^{-1}$ to the total phonon linewidth.}
elements.sent-2411-120: To better understand the computational results, we divide the total phonon linewidth into contributions from different electronic transitions, i.\,e.\,, with different $m$ and $n$ pairs.
elements.sent-2411-121: Also, this contribution does not disappear even if $|\mu|>\omega_\qnu/2$.
elements.sent-2411-122: However, the most interesting result is that the intra-band contributions, that is, processes involving the electronic transition in the same band ($m=n$), increase with $|\mu|$ and are dominant at high charge densities.
elements.sent-2411-123: This behavior is clearly observed in the Raman experiment by \citet{PhysRevB.91.205413 [see Fig.~9(a) thereof].}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{./Fig_Temp_variation.eps} \caption{ Linewidth of the zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonons of graphene at different temperatures calculated according to Eq.~\eqref{eq:Gamma_graphene} with finite electron linewidths [Eqs.~\eqref{eq:ImSigma_graphene}] and without finite electron linewidths - we assumed 0.001~eV linewidths. Here, we did not consider the increase in the electron linewidth with temperature. } \label{fig:temp_variations}

elements.sent-2411-125: We expect that the theory will be necessary to interpret experimental data on materials whose phonon energy scale is non-negligible compared to the energy scale of low-energy electronic transitions, which are materials of contemporary interest.

elements.sent-2411-136: If we account for the spin and valley degrees of freedom, the phonon linewidth [Eq.~\eqref{eq:Gamma ] of the degenerate zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonons of graphene reduces to \begin{widetext \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:Gamma_graphene} \Gamma_{E_{\rm 2g}} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi^2}\left(\frac{b\,\bar{g}}{\hbar\,v_{\rm F}}\right)^2 \sum_{m,\,n=\,\{-1,+1\}}\,\int_0^{4~{\rm eV}} d\varepsilon\,\varepsilon\,{\rm Re\,}\left\{...\right\}\,,\end{eqnarray} \end{widetext where $b$ is the carbon-carbon bond length in graphene, $v_{\rm F}$ is the band velocity around the Dirac point, and $\bar{g}$ is the angle-averaged electron-phonon coupling constant and is given by $\bar{g =\frac{3 {2}\,\eta\,\sqrt{\frac{\hbar {2M_{\rm C}\omega_\Gamma^{{\rm E _{2g }$ using the notation in Tab.~I of Ref.~\citealp{2014Park_NL_graphene_resistivity .}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{./Fig_10K_experiment_with_schematic.eps} \caption{ (a) Linewidth [full width at half maximum (FWHM)] of the zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonons of graphene vs. chemical potential at $T=10$~K. Theoretical results were obtained according to Eq.~\eqref{eq:Gamma_graphene} with finite electron linewidths [Eq.~\eqref{eq:ImSigma_graphene}] (solid or red curve) or with infinitesimal electron linewidths - we assumed 0.001~eV linewidths - (dashed or blue curve) from Eq.~\eqref{eq:Pi_omega3}. We took the experimental data from Ref.~\citealp{2007Yan_PRL_graphene_phonon_linewidth} and shifted them by $(-3)$~cm$^{-1}$ to account for other phonon scattering mechanisms than electron-phonon interactions (see text). (b) Schematic showing that a zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonon in graphene can decay through electronic transitions at low temperatures only if $|\mu|<\omega_\qnu/2$ if the electron linewidths are infinitesimal. { However, if finite electron linewidths are considered, the optical phonon can decay even when $|\mu|>\omega_\qnu/2$.} } \label{fig:graphene_experiment}
elements.sent-2411-138: At low temperatures, if $|\mu|<\omega_\qnu/2=0.1$~eV, a phonon with energy $\omega_\qnu=0.2$~eV can decay by an electronic transition; however, if $|\mu|>\omega_\qnu/2$, this transition is Pauli-blocked, and the phonon is not absorbed by vertical electronic transitions.
elements.sent-2411-139: We note that~\citet{2007Bonini_PRL_graphene_phonon have shown that three-phonon scattering processes at low temperatures add 1.65~cm$^{-1}$ to the total phonon linewidth.}
elements.sent-2411-140: This behavior is also clearly observed in the Raman experiment by \citet{PhysRevB.91.205413 [see Fig.~9(a) thereof].}
elements.sent-2411-141: Consider the electron-phonon interaction Hamiltonian within the second quantization formalism: Destruction of an electronic state and its subsequent creation is an allowed operation.
elements.sent-2411-142: Relaxation of the energy conservation can be intuitively understood as follows: Since there are uncertainties in the energies of the initial and final electronic states, we can find pairs of electronic states whose energy difference matches the energy of the $E_2g$ optical phonon.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{./Fig_Temp_variation.eps} \caption{ Linewidth of the zone-center $E_{\rm 2g}$ optical phonons of graphene at different temperatures calculated according to Eq.~\eqref{eq:Gamma_graphene} with finite electron linewidths [Eqs.~\eqref{eq:ImSigma_graphene}] and without finite electron linewidths - we assumed 0.001~eV linewidths. Here, we did not consider the increase in the electron linewidth with temperature { because that is not the subject of the paper. If that effect is considered, phonon linewidths may further increase at higher temperatures.} } \label{fig:temp_variations}

elements.sent-2411-147: Finally, one can think of the self-consistent determination of both electron and phonon linewidths.
elements.sent-2411-148: In fact, the theory of computing electron linewidths self-consistently assuming zero phonon linewidths was developed by \citet{2024LihmSCEL , and the theory of computing electron linewidths, assuming finite phonon linewidths but zero electron linewidths was developed by \citet{Lihm2024PlPh [see Eq. (S71) therein].}}
elements.sent-2411-149: However, the theory of computing electron linewidths assuming both finite electron linewidths and finite phonon linewidths has yet to be developed.
elements.sent-2411-150: In addition to this problem, there are other scattering mechanisms for electrons and those for phonons beyond the electron-phonon interactions that need to be taken into account, which, in principle, can be done, for the self-consistent computation of electron and phonon linewidths.

elements.sent-2411-153: We will make use of the following identity frequently: \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:Matsubara_sum_digamma} \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(ip_l + a)(ip_l + b)}\nnnl = \frac{\beta^2}{(2\pi i)^2} \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(l + \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\, a)(l + \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\,b )}\nnnl = \frac{\beta}{2\pi i} \frac{\psi(\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\,a) - \psi(\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\,b)}{a-b}\,,\end{eqnarray} which follows from \begin{equation}\label{eq:digamma_equality} \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(l+a)(l+b)} = \frac{\psi(a) - \psi(b)}{a-b}\,,\end{equation} which again follows from the definition of the digamma function in Eq.~\eqref{eq:digamma .}
elements.formula-2411-030: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Matsubara_sum_digamma} \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(ip_l + a)(ip_l + b)}\nnnl = \frac{\beta^2}{(2\pi i)^2} \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(l + \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\, a)(l + \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\,b )}\nnnl = \frac{\beta}{2\pi i} \frac{\psi(\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\,a) - \psi(\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{\beta}{2\pi i}\,b)}{a-b}\,,\end{equation}
